---
title: "Making an Author Index with Quarto"
subtitle: "From a seamless LaTeX workflow to a pandoc citeproc obstacle course"
author: "Michael Friendly"
date: "2026-05-04"
categories: [Quarto, LaTeX, publishing]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

<!-- DRAFT / SKETCH — fill in details, add code output examples, verify Perl snippets -->

A technical book in statistics or data science typically cites hundreds of papers
and books — methods developed by Tukey, visualizations introduced by Wilkinson,
models named after their inventors.
Readers who have encountered a method in the wild and want to find where it is
discussed in the book benefit enormously from an **author index**: a back-of-book
list of cited authors with the pages on which they are cited.
Most style guides for academic monographs recommend one; CRC Press / Chapman & Hall
require it.

This post describes how to generate an author index for a Quarto book — specifically,
what makes it trivial in a traditional LaTeX workflow and surprisingly hard in a
Quarto / pandoc workflow, and how I eventually solved it for
*Visualizing Multivariate Data and Models in R* (CRC Press, in preparation).

A companion post covers the [subject index](blog-quarto-indexes.qmd) — R helper
functions, LaTeX macros, and the underscore problem.

## How it works in a LaTeX-centric workflow

In my previous book,
[*Discrete Data Analysis with R*](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.1201/b19022/discrete-data-analysis-michael-friendly-david-meyer)
(CRC Press, 2016), chapters were written in `.Rnw` format — essentially LaTeX with
embedded R code chunks processed by `knitr`. The full compilation pipeline was:

```
.Rnw → knitr → .tex → LaTeX → BibTeX → LaTeX (×2) → authorindex Perl → LaTeX
```

The author index in that workflow is handled by two cooperating tools:

**The `authorindex` LaTeX package** (by Harris, 2006) provides a macro `\aicite{key}`
that you use instead of `\cite{key}` anywhere you want the cited author(s) indexed.
When LaTeX processes `\aicite{key}`, it writes a `\citationpage{key}{page}` entry
to the `.aux` file, in addition to producing the usual citation text.

**The `authorindex` Perl script** reads the `.aux` file to find all
`\citationpage{key}{page}` entries, then calls BibTeX to look up the author lists
for each key in your `.bib` files. For each citation it emits one
`\indexentry{LastName, First}{page}` line per author into an `.ain` file. LaTeX
then typesets this exactly like a regular index via `\printauthorindex`.

The `.bib` workflow makes everything available: `\bibdata{...}` in the `.aux`
tells the Perl script which databases to pass to BibTeX, and `\bibcite{key}{...}`
entries written by BibTeX map keys to formatted references. In `.Rnw` chapters
you use `\aicite{key}` wherever you would normally write `\cite{key}`, and
the author index builds automatically with no extra bookkeeping.

The result is a well-formatted two-column author index like this:

```
Blishen, B. R., 74, 88
Cleveland, W. S., 12, 45, 91–97
Friendly, M., 3, 7, 22, 44
...
```

## Why Quarto breaks all of this

Quarto uses **pandoc** and its built-in citation processor **citeproc** — a
fundamentally different pipeline:

```
.qmd → knitr → .md → pandoc (citeproc) → .tex → LaTeX (no BibTeX) → PDF
```

Every step of the `authorindex` workflow breaks:

### 1. No `\cite{}` commands in the generated `.tex`

Pandoc resolves all citations *before* LaTeX ever runs. Instead of emitting
`\cite{Friendly2013}`, it emits:

```latex
\citeproc{ref-Friendly2013}{Friendly (2013)}
```

The formatted text is already baked in. LaTeX just has to typeset it.
The `authorindex` package patches `\aicite` but has no hook into `\citeproc`.

### 2. No BibTeX step — no `.aux` infrastructure

Because pandoc reads the `.bib` files itself and writes the formatted
bibliography directly into the `.tex` file as a `thebibliography` environment,
LaTeX never calls BibTeX. So:

- `\bibdata{...}` is never written to `.aux` — the Perl script can't find the databases.
- `\bibcite{key}{...}` entries are never written — the Perl script can't map keys to authors.

### 3. Keys are renamed

Pandoc prefixes every citation key with `ref-`: the BibTeX entry `Friendly2013`
becomes `ref-Friendly2013` in the `.tex` and `.aux`. The `.bib` files still use
the original keys. The Perl script doesn't know about this prefix.

### 4. Windows CRLF line endings

The `authorindex` Perl script was written for Unix. Its regexes use `$` to match
end-of-line. On Windows, LaTeX writes `.aux` files with CRLF line endings
(`\r\n`), so `\r` sits before `\n` and `$` silently fails to match — every regex
that anchors to end-of-line returns nothing.

The net result: four independent failure modes, each one sufficient to produce
an empty or broken author index, with no obvious error messages.

## The solution path

### Step 1: Fix the Perl script

Two changes to `latex/authorindex` (working copy saved as `latex/authorindex_debug`):

**Strip CRLF.** At the top of the main input loop, add:

```perl
s/\r//g;    # strip Windows CR before any regex matching
```

**Strip the `ref-` prefix.** In the `\citationpage` and `\bibcite` handlers,
strip pandoc's prefix before downstream processing:

```perl
# in \citationpage handler:
my ($key, $page) = ($1, $2);
$key =~ s/^ref-//;          # pandoc prefixes all keys with ref-
```

**Soften the BibTeX exit-code check.** The `.bib` files in this project contain
package references that are duplicated across files. BibTeX warns "Repeated entry"
and exits non-zero, but still writes a usable `.bbl`. Change the hard `die` to a
warning when the `.bbl` file was produced anyway.

### Step 2: Write `\bibdata{}` to the `.aux` manually

Since BibTeX never runs, we have to tell the Perl script which `.bib` files to
use. A hook in `latex/preamble.tex` writes the entry at document start:

```latex
\AtBeginDocument{%
  \if@filesw\immediate\write\@auxout{%
    \string\bibdata{references,R-refs,pkgs,packages,Rpackages-4.5.1}}\fi
}
```

After LaTeX runs, `index.aux` contains `\bibdata{...}` and the Perl script
can call BibTeX with the right databases.

### Step 3: Write `\citationpage{}` entries for every citation

This is the hardest part. The `authorindex` package writes `\citationpage`
entries when `\aicite` is called — but pandoc never emits `\aicite`. We have
to intercept `\citeproc` instead.

**First attempt — patch `\@citex`:**

```latex
\AtBeginDocument{%
  \let\@ai@orig@citex\@citex
  \def\@citex[#1]#2{\@ai@orig@citex[#1]{#2}\@aicitey{#2}}%
}
```

This worked: `\citationpage` entries appeared in `.aux` and the Perl script
produced a correct `index.ain`. But it introduced a fatal new problem.

**The "Extra }, or forgotten `\endgroup`" deadlock:**

The original pandoc `\citeproc` wraps `\cite{key}` in `\begingroup...\endgroup`.
Inside a `\footnote{}`, when a citation is undefined on the first LaTeX pass
(which is normal — `\bibcite` entries are only written at the end of the document),
the group interaction causes:

```
! Extra }, or forgotten \endgroup.
l.4211 ...citeproc{ref-Blishen-etal-1987}{1987}).}
```

LaTeX stops mid-document, writing an incomplete `.aux`. The next build also has
undefined citations (because `\bibcite` entries weren't written on the truncated
pass) → same error → deadlock. The only escape is to delete the `.aux` file and
restore a backup.

**Why the patch is at fault:** The `\@citex` patch calls `\@aicitey{key}` from
inside `\@citex`, which is itself called inside `\citeproc`'s
`\begingroup...\endgroup`. Inside a `\footnote{}`, the group nesting is:

```
\footnote{
  \begingroup          ← from \citeproc
    \cite{key}
      \@citex          ← patched: calls \@aicitey here
                         group imbalance when key undefined
  \endgroup
}                      ← "Extra }"
```

**The fix — patch `\citeproc` directly:**

The group-imbalance only happens because `\@aicitey` is called inside
`\citeproc`'s `\begingroup`. The solution is to redefine `\citeproc` itself
so that it has *no* `\begingroup` — and call `\@aicitey` there:

```latex
% In preamble.tex — replaces pandoc's \citeproc definition:
\def\citeproc#1#2{\@aicitey{#1}#2}
```

This definition:

1. Calls `\@aicitey{#1}` to write `\citationpage{ref-key}{page}` to `.aux`.
2. Outputs `#2` — the pre-formatted citation text that pandoc already computed.
3. Has **no `\begingroup...\endgroup`** — no group imbalance is possible, even
   inside `\footnote{}` on the first pass.

Pandoc provides the correctly formatted text in `#2` regardless of whether the
citation is "defined" in the LaTeX sense, so the output is identical to the
original — no `\begingroup` is needed for formatting. This can be left enabled
permanently; no toggling is needed around builds.

Note: `\@aicitey` receives the key in pandoc's `ref-key` format. The Perl script
(Step 1) already strips the `ref-` prefix before looking up authors in the `.bib`.

### Step 4: Define LaTeX macros used in `.bib` files

My `references.bib` file contained an `@Preamble` directive defining a `\de{}`
command used in French author names (e.g., `{\de{O}}cagne`). In the `.Rnw`
workflow, BibTeX emitted preamble content into the `.bbl` file read by LaTeX.
With pandoc citeproc, `@Preamble` directives are silently discarded — they
never reach LaTeX — so `\de{}` was undefined when the author index was typeset.

Fix: define the command directly in `preamble.tex`:

```latex
\providecommand{\de}[1]{d'#1}
```

## The workflow

After all fixes are in place, generating the author index is a three-step process:

```bash
# 1. Build the PDF (TeXStudio or Quarto) — populates index.aux with
#    \citationpage entries and \bibdata

# 2. Generate index.ain
bash make-authorindex.sh

# 3. Rebuild the PDF — \printauthorindex typesets from index.ain
```

`make-authorindex.sh` sets `BIBINPUTS` and `BSTINPUTS` to point at the project's
`.bib` directory and the TinyTeX BST path, then runs `perl latex/authorindex -d index`.

`latex/after-body.tex` prints the index in a two-column, single-spaced layout:

```latex
\renewenvironment{theauthorindex}{%
  \linespread{1}\selectfont%
  \setlength{\parskip}{0pt}%
  \raggedright%
  \setlength{\leftskip}{1em}%
  \setlength{\parindent}{-1em}%
  \def\item[##1]{\par\hspace{0mm}\ainame{##1}}%
  \aisize}{\par}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Author Index}
\chapter*{Author Index}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\printauthorindex
\end{multicols}
```

## Lessons learned

1. **Pandoc citeproc is a black box to LaTeX.** The entire BibTeX/bibcite
   infrastructure that indexing tools rely on simply doesn't exist in the
   pandoc pipeline. Any tool that depends on `.aux` entries from BibTeX needs
   a workaround.

2. **Patch the highest-level macro you control.** Patching `\@citex` (a LaTeX
   internal) dragged in group-balance constraints that caused deadlocks. Patching
   `\citeproc` (a pandoc-generated macro that we were already redefining) was
   simpler and had no dangerous interactions.

3. **Group imbalance in footnotes is a deadlock, not just an error.** The first
   failed build truncates `.aux`; the next build then fails for a different reason
   (stale citations); the cycle continues until the `.aux` is manually deleted.
   Always test citation-patching code with a footnote citation before committing.

4. **Windows line endings break Unix Perl scripts silently.** Every regex that
   anchored to `$` returned nothing. The fix (`s/\r//g`) is one line but costs
   hours to diagnose.

5. **`@Preamble` in `.bib` files doesn't reach LaTeX via citeproc.** Any LaTeX
   macros defined there must be moved to `preamble.tex`.

---

*Files discussed in this post:*

- `latex/preamble.tex` — `authorindex` package setup and `\citeproc` redefinition
- `latex/authorindex` — the Perl script (original + CRLF / `ref-` fixes)
- `latex/after-body.tex` — `\printauthorindex` call and environment redefinition
- `make-authorindex.sh` — shell script with correct `BIBINPUTS`/`BSTINPUTS`
